claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 3 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 8 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 10 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 18 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 3 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit! Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child's age:**

How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know, and I'll guide you through the next steps!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi, yes I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible. 

I actually have three children I'm asking about - Alex who is 3, Blake who is 8, and Charlie who is 10.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those details! It's great that you're looking into Child Benefit for all three of your children.

Since Alex (3), Blake (8), and Charlie (10) are all **under 16 years old**, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. This is good news!

Now let me navigate to the next step in the assessment, which will apply to all three children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The next question is about whether the children are in paid work:

**Are all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work at all?**

Given their ages (3, 8, and 10), I would assume none of them are in paid employment, but I just need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh no, none of them work at all - they're just kids! Alex is only a toddler, and Blake and Charlie are still in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense - they're all young children. I'll confirm that none of them are working and move to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are all three children NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means none of them are claiming benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in their own name.

Given their young ages, this would be very unusual, but I need to confirm - are Alex, Blake, and Charlie all free from receiving such benefits themselves?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them receive any benefits in their own name. They're too young for that sort of thing.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly right! I'll confirm that and continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are any of your children currently in an apprenticeship?**

Given that Alex is 3, Blake is 8, and Charlie is 10 - and they're all in primary school or younger - I would expect the answer is no. But just to confirm: none of them are enrolled in any kind of apprenticeship programme, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - they're all way too young for apprenticeships! They're just little ones.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely! I'll confirm they're not in any apprenticeships and move on to the next part of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify through **any one** of the following:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're a resident of England, Scotland, Wales, or Northern Ireland
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can support yourself financially (without certain benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, **option 1 (normally living in the UK)** is the most common situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Living in the UK in Manchester means you meet the residency requirements. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check that you're responsible for the children.

**Are you responsible for Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

You can qualify through **any one** of these:

1. **Living with the children** - They live with you in your home
2. **Contributing to their upkeep** - You pay at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You make maintenance payments under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex, Blake, and Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex and Charlie live at home with me. But Blake doesn't live with me - he lives with his mum.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. So we have two different situations:

- **Alex (3) and Charlie (10)** - live with you ✓
- **Blake (8)** - lives with his mum

For **Alex and Charlie**, living with you means you're clearly responsible for them. That's straightforward.

For **Blake**, since he doesn't live with you, I need to ask:

**Do you contribute to Blake's upkeep in any way?** 

This could include:
- Paying maintenance (through a court order or agreement)
- Contributing money towards his food, clothes, pocket money, or housing
- Providing regular gifts or other support that equals at least the Child Benefit amount

Do you make any such contributions towards Blake's care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially towards Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being honest about that. This is important information for the assessment.

For **Blake**, since you neither live with him nor contribute to his upkeep, I'm afraid you wouldn't be considered "responsible" for him under the Child Benefit rules. His mum (who he lives with) would be the person eligible to claim Child Benefit for him.

However, let's continue the assessment for **Alex and Charlie**, as you do live with them. Let me proceed with the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about other potential claimants for **Alex and Charlie**.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie?**

For example:
- Another parent
- A partner or spouse
- A grandparent or other family member

If someone else is claiming, has there been an agreement about who should receive the benefit? Or is there any dispute about this?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that keeps things straightforward. Let me continue with the assessment for Alex and Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next, I need to check about Alex and Charlie's living situation.

**Are Alex and Charlie currently in any of the following situations?**

1. **In hospital** - Currently staying in hospital
2. **In care** - In local authority care
3. **Fostered** - Being fostered by someone else
4. **Living normally at home** - Just living at home with you in a normal arrangement

I assume they're both living normally at home with you in Manchester - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live at home with me normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost there! One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's or Charlie's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, are you supporting them independently without the local council contributing to their housing or living costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - the council isn't paying anything towards them. I'm supporting them myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That completes the assessment. Let me now provide you with the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Assessment Complete!

Here's the outcome of your Child Benefit eligibility assessment:

### ✅ **ELIGIBLE: Alex (age 3)**
Alex meets all the eligibility requirements - he's under 16, lives with you, and you're responsible for his care.

### ❌ **NOT ELIGIBLE: Blake (age 8)**
Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake because he lives with his mum and you're not currently contributing to his upkeep. Under the rules, you need to either live with the child OR contribute financially to their care. **Blake's mum would be the eligible person to claim Child Benefit for him.**

### ✅ **ELIGIBLE: Charlie (age 10)**
Charlie meets all the eligibility requirements - she's under 16, lives with you, and you're responsible for her care.

---

## 📋 Next Steps for Alex and Charlie:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and the children
3. **Provide birth certificates** for Alex and Charlie
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through tax
5. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

🔗 **To apply, visit:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

---

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the application process?

reasonings: Alex (age 3) meets all eligibility criteria: Under 16 years old (automatic age qualification), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, you meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), you are responsible for Alex (living with you), no other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 8) does not meet eligibility criteria because you are not considered 'responsible' for him under Child Benefit rules. Blake does not live with you (he lives with his mum) and you do not contribute financially to his upkeep. His mother, who he lives with, would be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie (age 10) meets all eligibility criteria: Under 16 years old (automatic age qualification), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, you meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), you are responsible for Charlie (living with you), no other person is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie, Charlie is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for two of your three children. Alex (3) and Charlie (10) both qualify as they live with you and meet all other eligibility requirements. However, you cannot claim for Blake (8) because he lives with his mum and you don't contribute to his upkeep - his mother would be the eligible claimant for him.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 3) meets all eligibility criteria: Under 16 years old (automatic age qualification), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, you meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), you are responsible for Alex (living with you), no other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 8) does not meet eligibility criteria because you are not considered 'responsible' for him under Child Benefit rules. Blake does not live with you (he lives with his mum) and you do not contribute financially to his upkeep. His mother, who he lives with, would be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie (age 10) meets all eligibility criteria: Under 16 years old (automatic age qualification), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, you meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), you are responsible for Charlie (living with you), no other person is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie, Charlie is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for two of your three children. Alex (3) and Charlie (10) both qualify as they live with you and meet all other eligibility requirements. However, you cannot claim for Blake (8) because he lives with his mum and you don't contribute to his upkeep - his mother would be the eligible claimant for him.
